messages.content: Create a guide on business email etiquette for new employees. Include proper use of honorific language and important considerations in Japanese business culture.

trialA.output:
# Business Email Etiquette Guide for New Employees ### Focus on Japanese Honorific Language & Cultural Norms --- ## 1. Why Email Etiquette Matters * **First‑impression** – A polished message signals professionalism and respect. * **Relationship‑building** – In Japanese business, *trust* is built through careful language. * **Avoiding misunderstandings** – Clear, polite wording reduces the risk of offence or error. --- ## 2. General (Global) Email Etiquette | Element | Best‑Practice Checklist | |---------|--------------------------| | **Subject** | • Keep it short (≈ 4–7 words)
• Include a clear cue (e.g., “Quote request – Project X”) | | **Greeting** | • Use the recipient’s title/name (e.g., “Dear Mr. Tanaka”)
• In Japanese‑focused mail, use “様” after the name | | **Opening line** | • State the purpose of the email in the first sentence
• Add a brief polite phrase (“Thank you for your continued support”) | | **Body** | • One idea per paragraph
• Use bullet points for lists
• Keep paragraphs ≤ 3–4 lines | | **Closing** | • Summarize any required action
• Add a polite sign‑off (“Thank you,” “Best regards”) | | **Signature** | • Full name, job title, department, company, phone, website
• No decorative graphics | | **Attachments** | • Mention the attachment in the body (“Please see the attached file”)
• Verify the file is actually attached | | **Timing** | • Send during business hours (09:00‑18:00 JST)
• If urgent, note “お忙しいところ恐れ入ります” (Sorry for disturbing you) | | **Proof‑read** | • Check spelling, grammar, tone
• Ensure correct honorifics (especially in Japanese) | --- ## 3. Japanese Business Email Specifics ### 3.1 Levels of Politeness (Keigo) | Form | When to Use | Example | |------|--------------|---------| | **Teineigo ( polite “‑ます” / “‑です”)** | Standard external communication | いただいております | | **Sonkeigo (respectful language)** | Referring to the recipient’s actions | をお届けいたします | | **Kenjogo (humble language)** | Describing your own actions | 動きがございましたら、ご連絡ください | > **Tip:** In most emails, **teineigo** is sufficient. Use *sonkeigo* and *kenjogo* only when you need extra formality (e.g., to senior executives or external VIPs). ### 3.2 Honorific Prefixes & Suffixes | Prefix/Suffix | Use | Example | |---------------|-----|---------| | **様 (‑sama)** | Most formal, used for individuals | 山田 様 | | **さん (‑san)** | Polite, common in internal mail | 田中 さん | | **殿 (‑tono)** | Very formal (rare, used for ceremonial or very senior people) | 社长 殿 | | **御中 (‑shichū)** | When addressing a department or company (no individual name) | ○○株式会社 御中 | | **各位 (‑kakui)** | Group of recipients (e.g., “All staff”) | 各位 | | **御社 (go‑sha) / 貴社 (ki‑sha)** | Polite “your company” | 御社のご都合は如何ですか？ | | **お / ご (o‑/go‑)** | Respectful prefixes attached to nouns (お名前, 、ご回答) | お忙しいところ | > **Note:** *御社* is more formal than *貴社*. Use *御社* when you want to show extra respect. ### 3.3 Typical Structure of a Japanese Business Email | Part | Japanese Phrase (common) | English Approximation | |------|---------------------------|-----------------------| | **Subject** | 件名: 【お見積もり】○○プロジェクトの件 | Subject: 【Quotation】Re: ○○ Project | | **Greeting** | 拜啓　○○様 | Dear Mr./Ms. ○○, | | **Opening** | 突然のご連絡失礼いたします。 | I apologize for contacting you without prior notice. | | | お忙しいところ恐れ入ります。 | Thank you for taking the time despite your busy schedule. | | **Context** | さて、このたびは○○の件につきまして、贵社へお願い申し上げたくメールいたしました。 | Regarding the ○○ matter, we would like to request your assistance. | | **Body** | • 詳細 (details)
• 必要事項 (requirements)
• 締切 (deadline) | • Details
• Requirements
• Deadline | | **Closing** | ご検討よろしくお願い申し上げます。 | I would appreciate your consideration. | | | 引き続きよろしくお願いいたします。 | Thank you for your continued support. | | **Signature** | ○○（名前）
○○株式会社 ○○部
電話: 03‑XXXX‑XXXX
メール: name@company.co.jp | (Your name)
Department, ○○ Co., Ltd.
Tel: …
Email: … | ### 3.4 Sample Email (Japanese + English) > **Subject:** お見積もり依頼 – ○○システム導入の件 > **From:** Taro Yamada > **To:** Kenji Suzuki – Suzuki Corporation ``` 拜啓　鈴木様 突然のご連絡失礼いたします。 お忙しいところ恐れ入ります。 このたびは、○○システムの導入につきまして、 御社へお願い申し上げたく、ご連絡いたしました。 ■ 目的 新規店舗へのPOSシステムの導入 ■ 必要機能 1. 売上管理 2. 在庫管理 3. 顧客管理 ■ 희망 提供希望日 2024年12月1日 附件に詳細仕様書（PDF）を差し上げております。 ご検討いただきありがとうございます。 ご返答を賜れますれば、幸いでございます。 引き続きよろしくお願いいたします。 敬具 山田 太郎 株式会社ABC　営業部 電話: 03‑1234‑5678 メール: taro.yamada@acme.co.jp ``` **English Translation (for reference)** ``` Dear Mr. Suzuki, I apologize for contacting you without prior notice. Thank you for taking the time despite your busy schedule. We would like to ask for your assistance regarding the implementation of the ○○ system for our new store. ■ Purpose Implementation of a POS system for a new location ■ Required functions 1. Sales management 2. Inventory management 3. Customer management ■ Desired delivery date December 1, 2024 Please find the detailed specification (PDF) attached. Thank you for your consideration. We would be grateful for your response. Best regards, Taro Yamada Sales Dept., ABC Co., Ltd. Tel: 03‑1234‑5678 Email: taro.yamada@acme.co.jp ``` --- ## 4. Important Cultural Considerations | Aspect | What to Do | Why It Matters | |--------|------------|----------------| | **Hierarchy** | Address senior staff first; use *様* for them; copy their manager only when needed. | Shows respect for authority. | | **Timing** | Send during business hours (09:00‑18:00 JST). If you must send outside, add a note: “夜分恐れ入ります”。 | Avoids imposing on personal time. | | **Response Expectation** | Respond **the same day** (or within 24 h). If you need more time, acknowledge receipt and give a timeline. | Promptness = professionalism. | | **No Emojis / Slang** | Keep the tone formal; avoid Western emoticons, slang, or humor. | Humor can be misinterpreted; emoji may seem childish. | | **“Read‑receipt”** | Rarely used; instead ask politely: “ご確認戴ければ、ご返信ください”。 | Respect for recipient’s privacy. | | **Cc / Bcc** | Use Cc for people who need to be informed; use Bcc for large mailing lists (protect addresses). | Shows consideration for privacy. | | **Proof‑read** | Double‑check honorifics, names, and company titles. A single mistake can be seen as disrespectful. | Attention to detail reflects credibility. | | **Attachment Check** | Mention the attachment in the body; verify it’s actually attached. | Missing files cause confusion and delay. | | **Use of “Thank you”** | In Japanese, “お礼申し上げます” or “感謝いたします” is more formal than simply “ありがとう”. | Shows deeper appreciation. | --- ## 5. Quick‑Reference Checklist (Japanese Email) - [ ] **Subject** clear & prefixed with “件名:” - [ ] **Greeting** uses “様” (or “御中” for a department) - [ ] Opening line includes a polite apology or acknowledgment - [ ] Body uses bullet points / numbered lists when possible - [ ] **Closing** ends with “ご検討よろしくお願い申し上げます” or similar - [ ] **Signature** includes name, title, department, company, phone, email - [ ] Attachment mentioned (“添付ファイルをご参照辱ください”) - [ ] No emojis, slang, or excessive casual language - [ ] Proof‑read for correct *keigo* (‑ます/‑です) - [ ] Sent during business hours (or noted otherwise) --- ## 6. Useful Phrase Cheat‑Sheet | Situation | Japanese Phrase | Romanized | Meaning | |-----------|----------------|-----------|---------| | **Apologize for intrusion** | 突然のご連絡失礼いたします。 | Totsuzen no renraku shitssuite orimasu. | “I apologize for contacting you without notice.” | | **Thank you for your time** | お忙しいところ恐れ入ります。 | O‑isashimi tokoro osore irimasu. | “Thank you for your time despite being busy.” | | **Request** | ご検討よろしくお願いいたします。 | Go‑kentō onegaishitashimasu. | “I would appreciate your kind consideration.” | | **Ask for confirmation** | ご確認の上、ご返信ください。 | Go‑kakunin no ue, go‑henji kudasai. | “Please confirm and reply.” | | **Closing** | 引き続きよろしくお願いいたします。 | Hanzoku ni yoroshiku onegaishimasu. | “Thank you for your continued support.” | | **Polite “your company”** | 御社 / 貴社 | Go‑sha / Ki‑sha | “Your company” (formal) | | **Polite “my company”** | 私ども / 当社 | Watakushi‑domo / Tōsha | “Our company” (humble) | | **Attachment note** | 添付ファイルをご参照辱ください。 | Tenpu fairu wo go‑sankō onegaishimasu. | “Please see the attached file.” | | **Formal sign‑off** | 敬具 | Keigu | “Sincerely” (used in letters, less common in email) | --- ### Final Thought Even if you’re writing in English, **adding a Japanese honorific or a short Japanese greeting** (e.g., “お忙しいところ恐れ入ります”) can dramatically increase goodwill with Japanese clients or colleagues. The key is **consistency, politeness, and attention to detail**—the same principles that underpin Japanese business culture overall. Good luck with your new role, and may your emails always be clear, respectful, and effective! 🚀

trialB.output:
**Business Email Etiquette Guide for New Employees**
*Navigating Japanese Corporate Communication with Confidence*

---

## 1. The Basic Structure (基本構成)

Japanese business emails follow a rigid structure that demonstrates respect and professionalism. Unlike Western emails, **context and relationship hierarchy** take precedence over brevity.

### Standard Format:
```
[Subject Line] 件名：Specific and concise
[Recipient] 〇〇株式会社 営業部 山田太郎 様
[Greeting] いつもお世話になっております。
[Opening] Preliminary remarks (前置き)
[Body] Main content
[Closing] 結びの言葉
[Signature] 署名（部門・名前・連絡先）
```

---

## 2. Mastering Honorific Language (敬語の使い分け)

Using the wrong keigo level is the most common mistake for new employees. Understand these three categories:

### A. 丁寧語 (Teineigo) - Polite Language
**Use with:** Colleagues at similar levels, external partners you work with regularly
- **です/ます** endings: 「資料を送ります」「確認です」
- **ご/お** prefixes: 「ご連絡」「お時間」

### B. 尊敬語 (Sonkeigo) - Respectful Language
**Use with:** Superiors, clients, customers (elevates the other person)
- **Special verbs:** 
  - 見る → **ご覧になる**
  - 言う → **おっしゃる**
  - 知る → **ご存知だ**
- **お/ご～になる:** 「お書きになる」「ご出席になる」

### C. 謙譲語 (Kenjōgo) - Humble Language
**Use with:** When describing your own actions toward superiors/clients (lowers yourself)
- **Special verbs:**
  - 見る → **拝見する**
  - 言う → **申す/申し上げる**
  - 知る → **存じる**
  - する → **いたす**
- **お/ご～する:** 「お送りする」「ご連絡する」

### **Critical Rule for New Employees:**
When in doubt, **over-use Kenjōgo** for your own actions and **Sonkeigo** for the recipient. It's better to be too polite than insufficiently respectful.

---

## 3. Subject Line Essentials (件名の鉄則)

Japanese subject lines must allow recipients to understand urgency and content without opening the email.

**Format:** `[Company Name] Re: Topic - Action Required`
- **Never** use vague subjects like 「お願い」 or 「質問」
- Include dates: 【12月15日まで】 or 【本日中】
- For replies, always preserve the subject chain: `Re: Re: [Original Subject]`

**Examples:**
- ✅ 弊社〇〇株式会社 12月契約書ご確認のお願い
- ✅ 【至急】【山田様】Aプロジェクト打ち合わせ日程調整
- ❌ お願いします
- ❌ 質問

---

## 4. Opening & Closing Protocols (挨拶と結び)

### First Contact (初回連絡)
```
〇〇株式会社 営業部 山田太郎 様

突然のメールで失礼いたします。
株式会社△△ 開発部の鈴木と申します。

[Body content]

お忙しいところ恐れ入りますが、
どうぞよろしくお願いいたします。
```

### Reply to Existing Contact
```
山田様

いつもお世話になっております。
株式会社△△の鈴木です。

[Body content]

ご確認のほど、よろしくお願いいたします。
```

### Seasonal Greetings (時候の挨拶)
Use these in the first line during specific periods:
- **January:** 新年あけましておめでとうございます
- **Summer:** 盛夏の候、ますますご清祥のこととお喜び申し上げます
- **Year-end:** 年末のご多忙中、大変恐縮ではございます

---

## 5. Critical Cultural Considerations (日本のビジネス文化における留意点)

### **The "Air Reading" (空気を読む) Principle**
- **Don't ask direct "yes/no" questions** if the answer might embarrass the recipient. Instead: 「恐れ入りますが、ご都合はいかがでしょうか」
- **Pre-emptive apologies:** Always apologize for potential inconvenience before making requests: 「お手数をおかけしますが」「恐縮ではございますが」

### **Hierarchy Awareness (縦社会の意識)**
- **Never send emails to skip-level superiors** without copying your direct manager
- When addressing multiple people, list names in order of seniority (highest first)
- Use **殿 (dono)** for senior executives in very formal situations instead of 様

### **The Nemawashi Warning**
Never use email for **first-time controversial proposals**. Japanese business culture requires face-to-face "nemawashi" (根回し) before formal email documentation. Sending surprise proposals via email is considered aggressive.

### **Reply Timing**
- **Internal:** Within 2-4 hours during business hours
- **External clients:** Within 24 hours, even if just to acknowledge: 「確認させていただき、明日までにご回答いたします」
- **Never** use read receipts (既読通知) — they're considered intrusive

### **Late-Night Email Protocol**
Sending emails after 8 PM or on weekends implies you expect the recipient to work overtime.
- **To superiors:** Schedule emails for 9 AM the next business day
- **To subordinates:** Acceptable to send late, but add 「明日からで結構です」or send Monday morning instead of Sunday

---

## 6. Attachment & CC Etiquette

### Attachment Naming
Always include your company name and date:
- ✅ 20241215_△△株式会社_企画書.pdf
- ❌ 企画書.pdf

### CC/BCC Rules
- **CC (Carbon Copy):** Information sharing only—recipients aren't expected to reply
- **BCC:** Only for mass emails where privacy matters. Never BCC someone to hide them from the main recipient in business correspondence
- **Reply vs. Reply All:** Default to **Reply** unless specifically asked to include everyone

---

## 7. Forbidden Phrases & Common Mistakes

### Never Use These:
| ❌ Casual/Wrong | ✅ Correct Business Form |
|---|---|
| わかりました | かしこまりました / 承知いたしました |
| 了解です | 承知いたしました |
| やっておきます | 対応させていただきます |
| ～してね | ～していただけますでしょうか |
| ちなみに | なお / また |
| 参考までに | ご参考までに |

### Grammar Pitfalls
- **「させていただきます」** is humble, but **「していただきます」** is respectful (asking someone else to do it). Don't confuse them.
- Avoid **「ございます」** after na-adjectives: Say 「簡単です」not 「簡単でございます」 (archaic/overly formal)

---

## 8. The Final Checklist

Before clicking "Send," verify:

**□ Recipient Check**
- Is the name spelled correctly? (Japanese names have multiple readings)
- Is the suffix appropriate? (様 for external, 殿 for very senior, さん for internal casual)

**□ Honorific Level**
- Did I use kenjōgo for my actions?
- Did I use sonkeigo for the recipient's actions?

**□ Cultural Safety**
- Did I apologize before making requests?
- Is the tone appropriate for the relationship depth?
- Did I avoid being too direct?

**□ Practical Details**
- Is the subject line specific?
- Are attachments named properly?
- Is my signature complete (company, dept, name, phone)?

---

## Final Advice for New Employees

**When you receive an email from a superior or client, mirror their language level.** If they use 「です/ます」, match that. If they drop keigo slightly, you may follow suit, but **never** be the first to lower the politeness level.

Remember: In Japanese business culture, **how you say something matters as much as what you say.** An email is not just communication—it's a demonstration of your respect for the organization and your professional maturity.

**When in doubt, ask your supervisor:** 「このメールの敬語レベルでよろしいでしょうか？」Most Japanese companies expect new employees to confirm email etiquette for the first 3-6 months.
